Modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding and
pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized midblock
crosswalks
Jing Zhao a,⇑, Jairus Odawa Malenje b, Jingxian Wu a, Ruoming Ma a
a University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai, China
b Masinde Muliro University of Science and Technology, Kakamega, Kenya
a r t i c l e
i n f o
Article history:
Received 17 February 2020
Received in revised form 5 May 2020
Accepted 25 June 2020
Available online 17 July 2020
Keywords:
Vehicle-pedestrian interaction
Crosswalks
Trafﬁc ﬂow model
Yielding
Gap acceptance
a b s t r a c t
The interaction between pedestrians and vehicles is an inevitable phenomenon at
unsignalized midblock crosswalks. This study aims to contribute to a better understanding
of the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance (VY and PGA). A
microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model was established to describe the interaction and explores its
effect on trafﬁc ﬂow. The VY and PGA behaviors were converged into the proposed model.
The proposed model was accomplished in a time step simulation. The results stability and
descriptive power of the proposed model were analyzed. The proposed model was also val-
idated using empirical data. The effects of the trafﬁc and geometric factors on the operation
of the unsignalized midblock crosswalks were discussed based on numerical experiments.
Accordingly, the recommendations on choosing the proper control mode of midblock
crosswalks (unsignalized or signalized) were proposed.

1. Introduction
The interaction between motor vehicles and non-motorized trafﬁc at crosswalks is a normal occurrence in urban centers
as people move from one side of the road to the other. The economic and social activities bestriding the roads (such as stores,
hospitals, hotels, and educational institutions), the composition of vehicle trafﬁc (such as cars, buses, and trucks) and road
geometry affect the frequency of these interactions. Where there is no signalized trafﬁc control, these activities can generate
intermittent pedestrian trafﬁc crisscrossing the road segment and interfering with vehicle trafﬁc ﬂow.
Most people may not be willing to walk a longer distance to the signalized crosswalks at intersections if crossing the
street where they are will give them convenient and direct access to their destination (Islam, Serhiyenko, Ivan,
Ravishanker, & Garder, 2014; Kadali & Vedagiri, 2013; Zhao, Ma, & Li, 2017). Urban transportation planners use the midblock
crosswalks to provide a convenient and orderly utilization of road facilities between the vehicles and pedestrians (Tezcan,
Elmorssy, & Aksoy, 2019). To avoid a regular interruption on vehicular trafﬁc ﬂow, the unsignalized midblock crosswalks
are widely used, thus potentially leading to conﬂicts (Yang, Liu, Xu, & Xu, 2016). Whether a midblock crosswalk is properly
set and whether it should be controlled by the signal is a puzzle for regulatory agencies. It depends on the interaction
between vehicles and pedestrians (Kutela & Teng, 2019). The three related topics, namely pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle

yielding behavior, and microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model, have been separately studied. Although many microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow
models have been established to analyze various complex trafﬁc phenomena, the existing car-following models did not con-
sider the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance (VY and PGA) behaviors. The gap acceptance
and yielding are the important decisions made by pedestrians and vehicles when passing the unsignalized midblock cross-
walk. The motivation of the paper is to contribute to a better understanding of the vehicle-pedestrian interaction phe-
nomenon at unsignalized midblock crosswalks by converging the probabilistic models of VY and PGA into the car-
following model.
The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews the relevant studies. Section 3 establishes the microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow
model and the simulation program. Section 4 validates the results stability, descriptive power, and the accuracy of the pro-
posed model. Section 5 analyzes the sensitivity of inﬂuencing factors. The conclusions are drawn in Section 6.
2. Literature review
This study explores the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, where the two
parties share sections of a roadway with or without conﬂicts in a trafﬁc stream. The main actions that deﬁne this interaction
are the driver slowing down and yielding (or failing to yield) on one side while the pedestrian waits or accepts the available
gap and crosses. When crosswalks are unsignalized, decisions of whether a vehicle yields or pedestrians accept available
gaps rests on the judgment of both parties at that point in time when they need to share the resource without conﬂicts
(Zhang, Chen, & Wei, 2019). These decisions and subsequent actions are greatly inﬂuenced by the behavior of both parties.
In the existing studies, there are three related topics, namely pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle yielding behavior, and
microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model.
On the aspect of pedestrian gap acceptance, the behavior of pedestrians and how they perceive and respond to the evolv-
ing trafﬁc ecosystem at the unsignalized midblock crosswalk may be inﬂuenced by their character and environmental factors
(Chai, Shi, Wong, Er, & Gwee, 2016; Leden, Gårder, & Johansson, 2006; Papadimitriou, Yannis, & Golias, 2009; Wang, Tan,
Schwebel, Shi, & Miao, 2018). Studies show that bold, assertive, and aggressive pedestrian crossing behavior as well as being
in big platoons can make drivers who may be otherwise unwilling to give way to yield (Tasic, Elvik, & Brewer, 2017; Varhelyi,
1998). The composition of pedestrians waiting group (based on gender, age groups, disability status and whether they bear
luggage) also affects their decision to either accept or reject a gap (Ferenchak, 2016). Reckless pedestrians tend to ‘‘comply”
with the need to wait if in a large group of waiting pedestrians but a large group also can embolden them to take a risky gap
believing in security in numbers (Elvik, 2016). Built-in safety features such as median island reservations, pedestrian refuges,
safety guardrails, markings on the road surface and sidewalks and clear visibility for pedestrians and drivers in the area of
the crossing, play a big part in the quality level of service for pedestrian facilities. An urban trafﬁc ecosystem with multilane
roads further presents a unique challenge to pedestrians. If they wait too long, then they may be impatient and choose the
rolling gap (Petzoldt, 2014) as the most convenient (but risky if trafﬁc speed is high) as they cross and stop one lane at a time.
Zhang, Zhou, Chen, and Chen (2017) proposed an ordered probit model of pedestrian-vehicle conﬂict analysis to explore the
effect of inﬂuencing factors, including trafﬁc volume, vehicle speed, pedestrian crossing behavior, pedestrian refuge, on
pedestrian-vehicle conﬂicts. A pedestrian dynamic gap acceptance model was further proposed to describe pedestrians’
crossing behaviors at any multi-lane crosswalk. (Zhang, Zhou, Qiu, & Liu, 2018). Kadali, Vedagiri, and Rathi (2015) the pre-
sent study adopts both the multiple linear regression and the artiﬁcial neural network with different pedestrian, trafﬁc and
vehicular characteristics to assess the signiﬁcant contributing factors for pedestrians’ gap acceptance behavior at unpro-
tected mid-block crosswalks under mixed trafﬁc conditions. It shows that pedestrian rolling behavior has a signiﬁcant effect
on pedestrian accepted gap size.
On the aspect of drivers, when they ﬁnd pedestrians at the crosswalks, their behavior is affected by their character, type of
vehicle, and the trafﬁc ecosystem (Goddard, Kahn, & Adkins, 2015). This midblock crosswalk encounter is also characterized
by subtle but non-verbal communication of intent between the two parties (Guéguen, Eyssartier, & Meineri, 2016). Both par-
ties in trafﬁc need to read and correctly perceive each other’s actions (such as hand signals, ﬂashing lights or eye contact) and
intentions to be able to act accordingly (Obeid, Abkarian, Abou-Zeid, & Kaysi, 2017; Sucha, Dostal, & Risser, 2017; Zhuang &
Wu, 2014). Merlino and Mondada (2019) explored the interaction in terms of different forms of mobility, distinct rights and
obligations of pedestrians, the practices, and the visual resources such as gaze/eye-contact and gestures through which
pedestrians execute the sequential and temporal trajectories of the crossing. According to existing studies, visible enforce-
ment of trafﬁc laws has been found to have a positive effect on driver yielding (Houten, Malenfant, Huitema, & Blomberg,
2013). Also, drivers approaching the crosswalks at higher speeds (Bertulis & Dulaski, 2018; Wang, Jiang, Wang, & Cheng,
2016) or in a platoon were less likely to give way to pedestrians (Hatﬁeld, Fernandes, Job, & Smith, 2007). On the other hand,
drivers are likely to be more careful in the presence of many pedestrians and, particularly, the vulnerable children and
elderly. The conspicuity of a crosswalk can be enhanced by many measures, such as placing elevated signs (Costa,
Bonetti, Vignali, Lantieri, & Simone, 2018; Costa et al., 2014), installing raised pedestrian crosswalks (Gitelman, Carmel,
Pesahov, & Chen, 2017), setting the median refuge island and ‘‘Yield here to pedestrians” ﬂashing vertical sign (Vignali
et al., 2019), and using automatic pedestrian detection (Høye & Laureshyn, 2019). Bella, Natale, and Silvestri (2017) further
analyzed drivers’ behavior while approaching pedestrian crosswalks to assess the effectiveness of Advanced Driving Assis-
tance Systems for pedestrian detection among several road environments.

223

Some other studies also focused on exploring the severity of conﬂicts. Lee and Abdel-Aty (2005) estimated the likelihood
of pedestrian injury severity when pedestrians are involved in crashes using an ordered probit model based on the vehicle–
pedestrian crashes at intersections in Florida over 4 years. They also developed a logical measure of exposure to better reﬂect
pedestrian crash risk by using the information on individual walking trips in the household travel survey. Almodfer, Xiong,
Fang, Kong, and Zheng (2016) proposed a lane-based approach for evaluating the post-encroachment time between pedes-
trians and vehicles. The lane-based distribution of pedestrian-vehicle conﬂict was analyzed. Vedagiri and Kadali (2016)
explored the severity of pedestrian-vehicle conﬂicts at unsignalized midblock crosswalks under mixed trafﬁc conditions
by considering the pedestrian-vehicle conﬂicts based on a pedestrian safety margin deﬁned as the time difference between
the time gap of an approaching vehicle and the pedestrian crossing time. Ni, Wang, Sun, and Li (2016) explored the evalu-
ation of pedestrians’ safety with attention to the effects of behavioral factors. Three interaction patterns, namely hard inter-
action, no interaction, and soft-interaction were considered. However, none of these studies converged the interaction into
the microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model, which makes it difﬁcult to conduct further study, such as analyzing the impact on trafﬁc
ﬂow
On the aspect of the microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model, it is a useful method to depict, reproduce, and predict the actual traf-
ﬁc phenomena by describing the dynamic characteristics of trafﬁc motion (Gupta & Katiyar, 2006; Yu, Tang, & Xin, 2018; Zhu
& Zhang, 2018). The car-following model is one of the most important parts in the ﬁeld of microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow. It can be
divided into the stimulus-response model, collision avoidance model, driving psychophysical model, and artiﬁcial
intelligence-based model (Li, Jiang, & Jia, 2011). Among them, the optimal velocity model (Helbing & Tilch, 1998; Jiang,
Wu, & Zhu, 2001; Nagatani, 1999; Zhao & Gao, 2005) has received extensive attention due to its analyzable and easy to sim-
ulate. Jiang et al. (2001) developed the full velocity difference (FVD) car-following model to address the problems of too high
acceleration and unrealistic deceleration in the optimal velocity model (Bando et al., 1995) and GFM (Helbing & Tilch, 1998)
by introducing positive relative velocity into the GF model to improve the trafﬁc ﬂow. Subsequently, other studies that have
extended the FVD model considered the anticipation optimal velocity (Peng & Cheng, 2013), driver’s anticipation behavior
(Kang, Sun, & Yang, 2015; Tang, Li, & Huang, 2010), timid and aggressive driving behaviors (Peng, He, & Lu, 2016), multiple
information of preceding cars (Peng & Sun, 2010), roadside memorial (Tang, Wu, Caccetta, & Huang, 2011), speed guidance at
intersections (Zhao & Li, 2016), trafﬁc jerk effect (Cheng, Liu, & Ge, 2017) and lately, illegal pedestrians at midblock cross-
walks (Malenje, Zhao, Li, & Han, 2018). For the vehicle-pedestrian interaction, Pretto, Cybis, and Jacobsen (2011) presented
a pedestrian road-crossing model, which focused on the representation of the pedestrians’ behavior by combining force ﬁeld
and rule based approaches. Daganzo and Knoop (2016) explored the effect of the pedestrianized street on car trafﬁc. The
results show that the street’s capacity is inversely proportional to the square root of the pedestrian ﬂux for low pedestrian
ﬂuxes, and that pedestrians increase the cars’ free-ﬂow pace by an amount that is proportional to the pedestrian ﬂux.
Recently, Yang, Kurt, Redmill, and Özgüner (2017) presented a vehicle-pedestrian interaction simulator based on social force
model for intelligent vehicles to achieve better decision making and local path planning. The simulator can effectively reﬂect
the interaction behavior between vehicles and pedestrians. They proposed two pedestrian trajectory datasets, CITR dataset
and DUT dataset, which can be used to calibrate and verify the pedestrian motion models with the consideration of vehicular
inﬂuences (Yang, Li, Redmill, & Ozguner, 2019).
The three topics related to this problem, including pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle yielding, and car-following, have
been studied and analyzed but separately. Although lots of works have been conducted to investigate the decision-
making process of pedestrians and vehicles, we still cannot describe a full picture of the vehicle-pedestrian interaction phe-
nomenon at unsignalized midblock crosswalks. Therefore, this study converged the probabilistic models of VY and PGA into
the car-following model. Vehicles can react according to the gap acceptance decision of pedestrians, and vice versa. In the
way, the trafﬁc operation at unsignalized midblock crosswalks can be simulated with the consideration of the interaction
between vehicles and pedestrians.
3. Modeling
In this section, we extend the traditional FVD model with the consideration of the interaction between VY and PGA behav-
ior at midblock crosswalks. The proposed car-following model integrates the judgment behaviors of the drivers and
pedestrians.
3.1. Trafﬁc situation
In vehicle-pedestrian interactions at the unsignalized midblock crosswalk, drivers respond to the presence of pedestrians
by slowing down to create a gap that allows them to cross and pedestrians respond by either accepting or rejecting the gap.
Pedestrians can only cross if there is a suitable gap to cross without the risk of being knocked down by a speeding oncoming
vehicle. A suitable gap is the one where pedestrians can commence and complete crossing before the car in front of the trafﬁc
stream reaches the stop line of the crosswalk. Therefore, drivers approaching the crosswalk need to slow down (or yield) to
avoid potential conﬂicts with pedestrians. If there is a stream of crossing pedestrians, they not only slow down but have to
stop if the number of pedestrians is large. However, there is a threshold beyond which no pedestrian is expected to com-
mence crossing if they have not started crossing when the vehicle arrives at the crosswalk.

The behavior of drivers and pedestrians is expected to be stochastic and depends on the conditions at the crosswalks at a
particular time. In the model, each vehicle should decide whether to yield according to the logit model when it faces a cross-
walk (no vehicle exists between the crosswalk and the target vehicle). When there is no pedestrian or pedestrian is crossing,
the driver has no choice but to proceed and yield, respectively. Similarly, the pedestrians should decide whether to cross
according to the gap acceptance logit model when they face a vehicle. When a gap is rejected, the pedestrian should wait
until the vehicle has passed the crosswalk, then respond to the new gap available. When the vehicle yields forwardly or any-
one in the waiting crowds decide to cross, the decision will be remade. The interaction will be deﬁned by the interaction of
the two logit models in the car-following model according to the conditions in Fig. 1.
Generally, the car following model is a microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model that describes a typical urban trafﬁc ﬂow based on
the behaviors of individual drivers. It can be described generally by the following expression.

3.2. Pedestrian crossing and vehicle yielding behavior
The interaction between pedestrians and vehicles is a convergence between the gap acceptance and yielding behaviors at
the crosswalks.
The pedestrian gap acceptance behavior is guided by the judgment of the pedestrians according to the following four fac-
tors: gap size, crossing distance, pedestrians waiting time, and location of waiting pedestrians (curbside or median). These
factors are combined in the following logistic model established by Zhao, Malenje, Tang, and Han (2019) based on the data
collected from 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai, China, as shown in Eq. (9). The utility function for the gap accep-
tance of pedestrians, as shown in Eq. (10), can be replaced by any suitable models without changing the framework of the
proposed model, e.g., as the vehicle waiting time at the crosswalk increases, drivers are more likely to accept a shorter gap
between pedestrians to reduce delay. It can be added to Eq. (10) as an inﬂuencing factor.

3.3. Simulation
The proposed model is realized by a time step simulation in MATLAB(R2018a) to describe the interaction between VY and
PGA behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks. The process of the simulation is shown as follows.
Step 1: Input data, including the position of the crosswalk (xc), the length of the crosswalk (D), the number of trafﬁc lanes
(Nl), the existence of the median island (M), the presence of the e-police (EP), the arrival distributions of vehicles and pedes-
trians, the distribution of the threshold of the gap acceptance for pedestrians, the distribution of the walking speed for pedes-
trians, the distribution of the threshold of the yielding for vehicles, and the parameters used in the car-following model (j, k,
V1, V2, C1, C2, lc).

pedestrians. Initialize the threshold of the yielding (dn) for the newly generated vehicles. Initialize the threshold of the
gap acceptance (gi) and the walking speed (vi) for the newly generated pedestrians.
Step 3: Determining the behavior of pedestrians. For any pedestrian i who are crossing (i 2 C tð Þ), make a judgment
whether the crossing has been ﬁnished, as shown in Eq. (14). For any pedestrian i who are waiting (i 2 W tð Þ), make a judg-
ment whether the gap is accepted using Eq. (11). If the gap is accepted, the pedestrian is going to cross, as shown in Eq. (15).
Then, the state of the crosswalk (the existence of pedestrians crossing on the crosswalk) can be determined by Eq. (16).

4. Model validation
The results stability, descriptive power, and the accuracy of the proposed model are validated in this section. Firstly, the
effect of the length of the analysis period on the stability of the calculation results is validated to make a recommendation on
the length of the analysis period. Then, we determine whether the common interaction between vehicles and pedestrians
can be reﬂected by the simulation. Last, the accuracy of the results of the simulation is validated using empirical data.
The parameters used in this section are set as follows. The number of trafﬁc lanes is two. The start and end of the analysis
area are between 0 m and 500 m. The unsignalized midblock crosswalk is located at 300 m. The length of the crosswalk
equals 3.5 m multiply by the number of trafﬁc lanes. The discretized time step is 0.1 s. The average arrival rate of vehicles
and pedestrians are 250 veh/h/ln and 300 ped/h, respectively, with Poission distribution. The threshold of the gap acceptance
for pedestrians and the threshold of the yielding for vehicles are set to be 0.5. The average walking speed for pedestrians is
1 m/s within the range of 0.5 m/s and 1.5 m/s. The parameters of the car-following model, including j, k, V1, V2, C1, C2, and lc,
are set to be 0.41, 0.2, 6.75, 7.91, 0.13, 1.57, and 9, respectively (Tang, Chen, Yang, & Shang, 2015; Tang et al., 2011; Zhao & Li,
2016).
4.1. Stability of the calculation results
The stability of the calculation results is affected by the length of the analysis period, as shown in Fig. 2. The model ran ten
times for each given analysis period. It can be found that there was less dispersion in the average value under a given analysis
period with the increase of the analysis length. Moreover, the standard deviation is relatively small (0.181 and 0.216 for the
standard deviation of the waiting time of pedestrians and vehicles, respectively) when the length of the analysis period
reaches to 7200 s (2 h).
4.2. Descriptive power validation
Fig. 3 shows an instance of the number of dwelling vehicles changing in each time-step. The two different colors of the
running trail in Fig. 3(a) indicate the running trail of vehicles on different trafﬁc lanes. In this period, 15 pedestrians arrive, 10
of them pass the crosswalk directly while the other ﬁve pedestrians wait a while before crossing. For vehicles, the vehicular
delay depends on the remaining crossing time of the pedestrian when the vehicle arrives, which is related to the pedestrian
walking speed, the length of the crosswalk, and the position of the pedestrian when the vehicle arrives. As shown in Fig. 3(b),
for pedestrian 1, the corresponding vehicle does not yield because the vehicle is very close to the crosswalk. The pedestrian
only waits 1.6 s. For pedestrians 2 and 3, they have to wait because the ﬁrst two vehicles they met do not yield. However, the
following vehicle yields, and then they can cross. The subsequent vehicles have to queue and wait until they ﬁnish the cross-
ing. For pedestrian 5, he rejects the gap he meets. However, the vehicle decides to yield him. So, after waiting a short time,
the pedestrian notices the vehicle yield (the vehicle slows down and the gap increases) and then starts to cross. It shows that
the proposed model can describe the interaction between VY and PGA behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks.
4.3. Empirical data validation
The proposed model converged the probabilistic models of VY and PGA into the car-following model, which were devel-
oped separately in previous studies (Malenje et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). However, the accuracy of the separate behavioral
models may not lead to the accuracy of the combined model. Therefore, in this section, the proposed model is validated using
empirical data. The data were collected from two unsignalized midblock crosswalks in Shanghai, China, in a peak hour, as
shown in Fig. 4. Since the previous studies covered the study area, the same parameter values for VY and PGA were used
without recalibration. The arrival time and the time of starting to cross of pedestrians were recorded. Then, the average wait-
ing time of pedestrians can be measured as the indicator to evaluate the veridicality of the model. The average value of the
ten runs with 7200 s for each run is used as the result of simulation for comparison. The comparison results are shown in
Table 2. One can ﬁnd that the maximum relative error is less than 10%. Moreover, the Two-Related-Samples nonparametric
test (Wilcoxon signed-rank) shows that there is no signiﬁcant difference (p > 0.05) between the estimated and the surveyed
values, as shown in Table 3. It indicates that the ability of the proposed model to estimate the operational performance at the
unsignalized midblock crosswalk is acceptable.

Moreover, the simulation will run 10 times for each scenario tested below.

the two sub-ﬁgures in Fig. 5, one can ﬁnd that the vehicular delay increases more rapidly than the pedestrian waiting time
with the increase of the volume. It is because the arriving vehicles have to queue once the vehicle in front yields and cannot
depart simultaneously after pedestrians complete crossing. Additionally, the queue clearance takes time as vehicles have to
restart and move according to the car-following model. With the increase in volume, more vehicles have to wait in the
queue. Consequently, the vehicular delay increases more rapidly with the increase of the volume between 400 and 600
(see Fig. 5(a)). However, as shown in Fig. 5(b), pedestrians can depart simultaneously once a gap is available. Therefore,
although the increase of the vehicular volume decreases the available gap for crossing, the inﬂuence on the waiting time
of the pedestrians is slight if the driver willing to yield.
For the given parameters (collected from the ﬁeld survey in Shanghai), the vehicles tend to yield. Under this condition, the
difference in delay between vehicles and pedestrians is great, especially when the vehicular volume is high. The signal con-
trol is recommended when the vehicular volume is larger than 480 veh/h by using the vehicular delay of 20 s as a threshold,
which is the criteria of the LOS (level of service) B for vehicles at signalized intersections (TRB, 2010). Please note that in
Fig. 5, the volume is constant for each scenario. However, under the condition of the trafﬁc demand ﬂuctuation, e.g., during
different times of day, it is unsuitable for controlling the crosswalk by ﬁxed signal timing. A signal control with pushbutton is
an alternative scheme to increase ﬂexibility (Kutela & Teng, 2020).
5.2. Effect of the pedestrian volume
The presence and number of pedestrians at an unsignalized crosswalk do affect the response from drivers and inﬂuence
their decision to either yield or not. In this test, the inputs for the volume of the pedestrians measured as the cumulative
number of pedestrians per hour (ped/h) was set from 100 to 800 at 100 intervals. Other parameters are constant, which
is the same as that in Section 4. The result of this test shows that the delay of vehicles increases more rapidly with the
increase in the volume of pedestrians, as shown in Fig. 6(a). Notably, the more the pedestrian volume is, the more time
the crosswalk will be occupied by pedestrians. Then, vehicles have to remain in the queue for a longer time. Moreover, after

However, for pedestrians, the waiting time is inversely proportional to the volume of pedestrians. From pedestrians’ per-
spective, with the increase in the pedestrian volume, more time is available for use by pedestrians, as shown in Fig. 6(b). It is
because if one gap is accepted, the crosswalk will be occupied by the pedestrians for the duration of the crossing move, and
in the process, vehicles have to slow down and yield to pedestrians to use the crosswalk. If the pedestrians arrive continu-
ously, the crosswalk will be occupied for a long time. Therefore, along the same line as the vehicular volume analysis, the
signal control is recommended when the pedestrian volume is larger than 500 ped/h by using the vehicular delay of 20 s
as a threshold. E.g., the crosswalk can be controlled by the signal at the school area because the extreme peak crossing
demand may occur during the morning and afternoon school time (Tang, Xie, Wang, & Zhang, 2019). It can also ensure
the safety of the pedestrian.
5.3. Effect of the number of trafﬁc lanes
The number of lanes affects the distance and duration that pedestrians need to traverse a crosswalk. In this test, the num-
ber of trafﬁc lanes used was set to be 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6, when other parameters are constant, which is the same as that in
Section 4. Fig. 7 shows that, for the pedestrians, the increase in the number of trafﬁc lanes makes the waiting time to increase
rapidly. It is because of the sharp decline in the available gap. Moreover, the possibility that vehicles on all the trafﬁc lanes
yielding also drops substantially with the increase in the number of trafﬁc lanes. Therefore, it is challenging for pedestrians
to ﬁnd a safe gap for crossing under the condition that the number of trafﬁc lanes is high. According to Guo, Wang, Guo, Jiang,
and Bubb (2012), most pedestrians can endure up to a 50 s wait. Therefore, it is not appropriate to have an unsignalized
crosswalk set on a segment with more than 4 lanes. Instead, a central island should be added to reduce the number of trafﬁc
lanes the pedestrians have to cross in one time. Alternatively, the crosswalk can be signalized to clarify the right of way.
For the vehicles, the delay also increases with the increase in the number of trafﬁc lanes. On average, 4 s increases in
vehicular delay for one trafﬁc lane increase. It is because the more the number of trafﬁc lanes is, the longer time the pedes-
trians have to spend on the crossing.

6. Conclusion
This study explored the interaction of vehicles and pedestrians at the unsignalized midblock crosswalks, where the two
parties share sections of a roadway with or without conﬂicts in a trafﬁc stream based on the car-following theory. It involved
developing a microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model considering the behaviors of both the driver and pedestrians as represented in
established logit models of VY and PGA. The numerical results show that the proposed model can describe the interaction
and qualitatively analyze its effects on trafﬁc ﬂow. The operational performance of the unsignalized midblock crosswalks
under different trafﬁc and geometric conditions were explored based on the sensitivity analyses. The following are the
key ﬁndings of the study.
(1) For pedestrians, the waiting time increases with the increase in the number of lanes and vehicular volume but
decreases with the pedestrian volume. The number of lanes is the critical factor affecting the waiting time of the
pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks.
(2) The vehicular delay increases with the growth of the vehicles’ volume, the pedestrians’ volume, and the number of
lanes. Compared with the pedestrians, the vehicular delay increases more rapidly than the pedestrian waiting time
with the increase of the vehicles’ volume, pedestrians’ volume.
(3) The signal control is recommended when the pedestrian waiting time and vehicular delay is high. It is related to the
behavior of drivers and pedestrians. According to the parameters surveyed in this study, the signal control is recom-
mended when the number of trafﬁc lanes at the midblock crosswalk is more than 4.
This study explored the effect of vehicle-pedestrian interaction at a midblock unsignalized crosswalk where pedestrian
crossing is legal though not controlled. The interaction under the condition the crosswalk is not set at the segment is worth
to do further study in the future because the illegal crossing (cross at the point without the crosswalk) is typical in devel-
oping countries. It is a tradeoff whether a midblock crosswalk should be set. Moreover, the combined consideration of the
midblock crosswalk and the adjacent intersections can be the direction of the future study. The arrival distribution of vehi-
cles is strongly affected when the distance to adjacent intersections is considered.

Modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding and
pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized midblock
crosswalks
Jing Zhao a,⇑, Jairus Odawa Malenje b, Jingxian Wu a, Ruoming Ma a
a University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai, China
b Masinde Muliro University of Science and Technology, Kakamega, Kenya
a r t i c l e
i n f o
Article history:
Received 17 February 2020
Received in revised form 5 May 2020
Accepted 25 June 2020
Available online 17 July 2020
Keywords:
Vehicle-pedestrian interaction
Crosswalks
Trafﬁc ﬂow model
Yielding
Gap acceptance
a b s t r a c t
The interaction between pedestrians and vehicles is an inevitable phenomenon at
unsignalized midblock crosswalks. This study aims to contribute to a better understanding
of the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance (VY and PGA). A
microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model was established to describe the interaction and explores its
effect on trafﬁc ﬂow. The VY and PGA behaviors were converged into the proposed model.
The proposed model was accomplished in a time step simulation. The results stability and
descriptive power of the proposed model were analyzed. The proposed model was also val-
idated using empirical data. The effects of the trafﬁc and geometric factors on the operation
of the unsignalized midblock crosswalks were discussed based on numerical experiments.
Accordingly, the recommendations on choosing the proper control mode of midblock
crosswalks (unsignalized or signalized) were proposed.

1. Introduction
The interaction between motor vehicles and non-motorized trafﬁc at crosswalks is a normal occurrence in urban centers
as people move from one side of the road to the other. The economic and social activities bestriding the roads (such as stores,
hospitals, hotels, and educational institutions), the composition of vehicle trafﬁc (such as cars, buses, and trucks) and road
geometry affect the frequency of these interactions. Where there is no signalized trafﬁc control, these activities can generate
intermittent pedestrian trafﬁc crisscrossing the road segment and interfering with vehicle trafﬁc ﬂow.
Most people may not be willing to walk a longer distance to the signalized crosswalks at intersections if crossing the
street where they are will give them convenient and direct access to their destination (Islam, Serhiyenko, Ivan,
Ravishanker, & Garder, 2014; Kadali & Vedagiri, 2013; Zhao, Ma, & Li, 2017). Urban transportation planners use the midblock
crosswalks to provide a convenient and orderly utilization of road facilities between the vehicles and pedestrians (Tezcan,
Elmorssy, & Aksoy, 2019). To avoid a regular interruption on vehicular trafﬁc ﬂow, the unsignalized midblock crosswalks
are widely used, thus potentially leading to conﬂicts (Yang, Liu, Xu, & Xu, 2016). Whether a midblock crosswalk is properly
set and whether it should be controlled by the signal is a puzzle for regulatory agencies. It depends on the interaction
between vehicles and pedestrians (Kutela & Teng, 2019). The three related topics, namely pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle

yielding behavior, and microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model, have been separately studied. Although many microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow
models have been established to analyze various complex trafﬁc phenomena, the existing car-following models did not con-
sider the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance (VY and PGA) behaviors. The gap acceptance
and yielding are the important decisions made by pedestrians and vehicles when passing the unsignalized midblock cross-
walk. The motivation of the paper is to contribute to a better understanding of the vehicle-pedestrian interaction phe-
nomenon at unsignalized midblock crosswalks by converging the probabilistic models of VY and PGA into the car-
following model.
The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews the relevant studies. Section 3 establishes the microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow
model and the simulation program. Section 4 validates the results stability, descriptive power, and the accuracy of the pro-
posed model. Section 5 analyzes the sensitivity of inﬂuencing factors. The conclusions are drawn in Section 6.
2. Literature review
This study explores the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, where the two
parties share sections of a roadway with or without conﬂicts in a trafﬁc stream. The main actions that deﬁne this interaction
are the driver slowing down and yielding (or failing to yield) on one side while the pedestrian waits or accepts the available
gap and crosses. When crosswalks are unsignalized, decisions of whether a vehicle yields or pedestrians accept available
gaps rests on the judgment of both parties at that point in time when they need to share the resource without conﬂicts
(Zhang, Chen, & Wei, 2019). These decisions and subsequent actions are greatly inﬂuenced by the behavior of both parties.
In the existing studies, there are three related topics, namely pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle yielding behavior, and
microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model.
On the aspect of pedestrian gap acceptance, the behavior of pedestrians and how they perceive and respond to the evolv-
ing trafﬁc ecosystem at the unsignalized midblock crosswalk may be inﬂuenced by their character and environmental factors
(Chai, Shi, Wong, Er, & Gwee, 2016; Leden, Gårder, & Johansson, 2006; Papadimitriou, Yannis, & Golias, 2009; Wang, Tan,
Schwebel, Shi, & Miao, 2018). Studies show that bold, assertive, and aggressive pedestrian crossing behavior as well as being
in big platoons can make drivers who may be otherwise unwilling to give way to yield (Tasic, Elvik, & Brewer, 2017; Varhelyi,
1998). The composition of pedestrians waiting group (based on gender, age groups, disability status and whether they bear
luggage) also affects their decision to either accept or reject a gap (Ferenchak, 2016). Reckless pedestrians tend to ‘‘comply”
with the need to wait if in a large group of waiting pedestrians but a large group also can embolden them to take a risky gap
believing in security in numbers (Elvik, 2016). Built-in safety features such as median island reservations, pedestrian refuges,
safety guardrails, markings on the road surface and sidewalks and clear visibility for pedestrians and drivers in the area of
the crossing, play a big part in the quality level of service for pedestrian facilities. An urban trafﬁc ecosystem with multilane
roads further presents a unique challenge to pedestrians. If they wait too long, then they may be impatient and choose the
rolling gap (Petzoldt, 2014) as the most convenient (but risky if trafﬁc speed is high) as they cross and stop one lane at a time.
Zhang, Zhou, Chen, and Chen (2017) proposed an ordered probit model of pedestrian-vehicle conﬂict analysis to explore the
effect of inﬂuencing factors, including trafﬁc volume, vehicle speed, pedestrian crossing behavior, pedestrian refuge, on
pedestrian-vehicle conﬂicts. A pedestrian dynamic gap acceptance model was further proposed to describe pedestrians’
crossing behaviors at any multi-lane crosswalk. (Zhang, Zhou, Qiu, & Liu, 2018). Kadali, Vedagiri, and Rathi (2015) the pre-
sent study adopts both the multiple linear regression and the artiﬁcial neural network with different pedestrian, trafﬁc and
vehicular characteristics to assess the signiﬁcant contributing factors for pedestrians’ gap acceptance behavior at unpro-
tected mid-block crosswalks under mixed trafﬁc conditions. It shows that pedestrian rolling behavior has a signiﬁcant effect
on pedestrian accepted gap size.
On the aspect of drivers, when they ﬁnd pedestrians at the crosswalks, their behavior is affected by their character, type of
vehicle, and the trafﬁc ecosystem (Goddard, Kahn, & Adkins, 2015). This midblock crosswalk encounter is also characterized
by subtle but non-verbal communication of intent between the two parties (Guéguen, Eyssartier, & Meineri, 2016). Both par-
ties in trafﬁc need to read and correctly perceive each other’s actions (such as hand signals, ﬂashing lights or eye contact) and
intentions to be able to act accordingly (Obeid, Abkarian, Abou-Zeid, & Kaysi, 2017; Sucha, Dostal, & Risser, 2017; Zhuang &
Wu, 2014). Merlino and Mondada (2019) explored the interaction in terms of different forms of mobility, distinct rights and
obligations of pedestrians, the practices, and the visual resources such as gaze/eye-contact and gestures through which
pedestrians execute the sequential and temporal trajectories of the crossing. According to existing studies, visible enforce-
ment of trafﬁc laws has been found to have a positive effect on driver yielding (Houten, Malenfant, Huitema, & Blomberg,
2013). Also, drivers approaching the crosswalks at higher speeds (Bertulis & Dulaski, 2018; Wang, Jiang, Wang, & Cheng,
2016) or in a platoon were less likely to give way to pedestrians (Hatﬁeld, Fernandes, Job, & Smith, 2007). On the other hand,
drivers are likely to be more careful in the presence of many pedestrians and, particularly, the vulnerable children and
elderly. The conspicuity of a crosswalk can be enhanced by many measures, such as placing elevated signs (Costa,
Bonetti, Vignali, Lantieri, & Simone, 2018; Costa et al., 2014), installing raised pedestrian crosswalks (Gitelman, Carmel,
Pesahov, & Chen, 2017), setting the median refuge island and ‘‘Yield here to pedestrians” ﬂashing vertical sign (Vignali
et al., 2019), and using automatic pedestrian detection (Høye & Laureshyn, 2019). Bella, Natale, and Silvestri (2017) further
analyzed drivers’ behavior while approaching pedestrian crosswalks to assess the effectiveness of Advanced Driving Assis-
tance Systems for pedestrian detection among several road environments.
J. Zhao et al. / Transportation Research Part F 73 (2020) 222–235
223

Some other studies also focused on exploring the severity of conﬂicts. Lee and Abdel-Aty (2005) estimated the likelihood
of pedestrian injury severity when pedestrians are involved in crashes using an ordered probit model based on the vehicle–
pedestrian crashes at intersections in Florida over 4 years. They also developed a logical measure of exposure to better reﬂect
pedestrian crash risk by using the information on individual walking trips in the household travel survey. Almodfer, Xiong,
Fang, Kong, and Zheng (2016) proposed a lane-based approach for evaluating the post-encroachment time between pedes-
trians and vehicles. The lane-based distribution of pedestrian-vehicle conﬂict was analyzed. Vedagiri and Kadali (2016)
explored the severity of pedestrian-vehicle conﬂicts at unsignalized midblock crosswalks under mixed trafﬁc conditions
by considering the pedestrian-vehicle conﬂicts based on a pedestrian safety margin deﬁned as the time difference between
the time gap of an approaching vehicle and the pedestrian crossing time. Ni, Wang, Sun, and Li (2016) explored the evalu-
ation of pedestrians’ safety with attention to the effects of behavioral factors. Three interaction patterns, namely hard inter-
action, no interaction, and soft-interaction were considered. However, none of these studies converged the interaction into
the microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model, which makes it difﬁcult to conduct further study, such as analyzing the impact on trafﬁc
ﬂow
On the aspect of the microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model, it is a useful method to depict, reproduce, and predict the actual traf-
ﬁc phenomena by describing the dynamic characteristics of trafﬁc motion (Gupta & Katiyar, 2006; Yu, Tang, & Xin, 2018; Zhu
& Zhang, 2018). The car-following model is one of the most important parts in the ﬁeld of microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow. It can be
divided into the stimulus-response model, collision avoidance model, driving psychophysical model, and artiﬁcial
intelligence-based model (Li, Jiang, & Jia, 2011). Among them, the optimal velocity model (Helbing & Tilch, 1998; Jiang,
Wu, & Zhu, 2001; Nagatani, 1999; Zhao & Gao, 2005) has received extensive attention due to its analyzable and easy to sim-
ulate. Jiang et al. (2001) developed the full velocity difference (FVD) car-following model to address the problems of too high
acceleration and unrealistic deceleration in the optimal velocity model (Bando et al., 1995) and GFM (Helbing & Tilch, 1998)
by introducing positive relative velocity into the GF model to improve the trafﬁc ﬂow. Subsequently, other studies that have
extended the FVD model considered the anticipation optimal velocity (Peng & Cheng, 2013), driver’s anticipation behavior
(Kang, Sun, & Yang, 2015; Tang, Li, & Huang, 2010), timid and aggressive driving behaviors (Peng, He, & Lu, 2016), multiple
information of preceding cars (Peng & Sun, 2010), roadside memorial (Tang, Wu, Caccetta, & Huang, 2011), speed guidance at
intersections (Zhao & Li, 2016), trafﬁc jerk effect (Cheng, Liu, & Ge, 2017) and lately, illegal pedestrians at midblock cross-
walks (Malenje, Zhao, Li, & Han, 2018). For the vehicle-pedestrian interaction, Pretto, Cybis, and Jacobsen (2011) presented
a pedestrian road-crossing model, which focused on the representation of the pedestrians’ behavior by combining force ﬁeld
and rule based approaches. Daganzo and Knoop (2016) explored the effect of the pedestrianized street on car trafﬁc. The
results show that the street’s capacity is inversely proportional to the square root of the pedestrian ﬂux for low pedestrian
ﬂuxes, and that pedestrians increase the cars’ free-ﬂow pace by an amount that is proportional to the pedestrian ﬂux.
Recently, Yang, Kurt, Redmill, and Özgüner (2017) presented a vehicle-pedestrian interaction simulator based on social force
model for intelligent vehicles to achieve better decision making and local path planning. The simulator can effectively reﬂect
the interaction behavior between vehicles and pedestrians. They proposed two pedestrian trajectory datasets, CITR dataset
and DUT dataset, which can be used to calibrate and verify the pedestrian motion models with the consideration of vehicular
inﬂuences (Yang, Li, Redmill, & Ozguner, 2019).
The three topics related to this problem, including pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle yielding, and car-following, have
been studied and analyzed but separately. Although lots of works have been conducted to investigate the decision-
making process of pedestrians and vehicles, we still cannot describe a full picture of the vehicle-pedestrian interaction phe-
nomenon at unsignalized midblock crosswalks. Therefore, this study converged the probabilistic models of VY and PGA into
the car-following model. Vehicles can react according to the gap acceptance decision of pedestrians, and vice versa. In the
way, the trafﬁc operation at unsignalized midblock crosswalks can be simulated with the consideration of the interaction
between vehicles and pedestrians.
3. Modeling
In this section, we extend the traditional FVD model with the consideration of the interaction between VY and PGA behav-
ior at midblock crosswalks. The proposed car-following model integrates the judgment behaviors of the drivers and
pedestrians.
3.1. Trafﬁc situation
In vehicle-pedestrian interactions at the unsignalized midblock crosswalk, drivers respond to the presence of pedestrians
by slowing down to create a gap that allows them to cross and pedestrians respond by either accepting or rejecting the gap.
Pedestrians can only cross if there is a suitable gap to cross without the risk of being knocked down by a speeding oncoming
vehicle. A suitable gap is the one where pedestrians can commence and complete crossing before the car in front of the trafﬁc
stream reaches the stop line of the crosswalk. Therefore, drivers approaching the crosswalk need to slow down (or yield) to
avoid potential conﬂicts with pedestrians. If there is a stream of crossing pedestrians, they not only slow down but have to
stop if the number of pedestrians is large. However, there is a threshold beyond which no pedestrian is expected to com-
mence crossing if they have not started crossing when the vehicle arrives at the crosswalk.
224
J. Zhao et al. / Transportation Research Part F 73 (2020) 222–235

The behavior of drivers and pedestrians is expected to be stochastic and depends on the conditions at the crosswalks at a
particular time. In the model, each vehicle should decide whether to yield according to the logit model when it faces a cross-
walk (no vehicle exists between the crosswalk and the target vehicle). When there is no pedestrian or pedestrian is crossing,
the driver has no choice but to proceed and yield, respectively. Similarly, the pedestrians should decide whether to cross
according to the gap acceptance logit model when they face a vehicle. When a gap is rejected, the pedestrian should wait
until the vehicle has passed the crosswalk, then respond to the new gap available. When the vehicle yields forwardly or any-
one in the waiting crowds decide to cross, the decision will be remade. The interaction will be deﬁned by the interaction of
the two logit models in the car-following model according to the conditions in Fig. 1.
Generally, the car following model is a microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model that describes a typical urban trafﬁc ﬂow based on
the behaviors of individual drivers. It can be described generally by the following expression.

3.2. Pedestrian crossing and vehicle yielding behavior
The interaction between pedestrians and vehicles is a convergence between the gap acceptance and yielding behaviors at
the crosswalks.
The pedestrian gap acceptance behavior is guided by the judgment of the pedestrians according to the following four fac-
tors: gap size, crossing distance, pedestrians waiting time, and location of waiting pedestrians (curbside or median). These

proposed model, e.g., as the vehicle waiting time at the crosswalk increases, drivers are more likely to accept a shorter gap
between pedestrians to reduce delay. It can be added to Eq. (10) as an inﬂuencing factor.

3.3. Simulation
The proposed model is realized by a time step simulation in MATLAB(R2018a) to describe the interaction between VY and
PGA behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks. The process of the simulation is shown as follows.
Step 1: Input data, including the position of the crosswalk (xc), the length of the crosswalk (D), the number of trafﬁc lanes
(Nl), the existence of the median island (M), the presence of the e-police (EP), the arrival distributions of vehicles and pedes-
trians, the distribution of the threshold of the gap acceptance for pedestrians, the distribution of the walking speed for pedes-
trians, the distribution of the threshold of the yielding for vehicles, and the parameters used in the car-following model (j, k,
V1, V2, C1, C2, lc).

4. Model validation
The results stability, descriptive power, and the accuracy of the proposed model are validated in this section. Firstly, the
effect of the length of the analysis period on the stability of the calculation results is validated to make a recommendation on
the length of the analysis period. Then, we determine whether the common interaction between vehicles and pedestrians
can be reﬂected by the simulation. Last, the accuracy of the results of the simulation is validated using empirical data.
The parameters used in this section are set as follows. The number of trafﬁc lanes is two. The start and end of the analysis
area are between 0 m and 500 m. The unsignalized midblock crosswalk is located at 300 m. The length of the crosswalk
equals 3.5 m multiply by the number of trafﬁc lanes. The discretized time step is 0.1 s. The average arrival rate of vehicles
and pedestrians are 250 veh/h/ln and 300 ped/h, respectively, with Poission distribution. The threshold of the gap acceptance
for pedestrians and the threshold of the yielding for vehicles are set to be 0.5. The average walking speed for pedestrians is
1 m/s within the range of 0.5 m/s and 1.5 m/s. The parameters of the car-following model, including j, k, V1, V2, C1, C2, and lc,
are set to be 0.41, 0.2, 6.75, 7.91, 0.13, 1.57, and 9, respectively (Tang, Chen, Yang, & Shang, 2015; Tang et al., 2011; Zhao & Li,
2016).
4.1. Stability of the calculation results
The stability of the calculation results is affected by the length of the analysis period, as shown in Fig. 2. The model ran ten
times for each given analysis period. It can be found that there was less dispersion in the average value under a given analysis
period with the increase of the analysis length. Moreover, the standard deviation is relatively small (0.181 and 0.216 for the
standard deviation of the waiting time of pedestrians and vehicles, respectively) when the length of the analysis period
reaches to 7200 s (2 h).
4.2. Descriptive power validation
Fig. 3 shows an instance of the number of dwelling vehicles changing in each time-step. The two different colors of the
running trail in Fig. 3(a) indicate the running trail of vehicles on different trafﬁc lanes. In this period, 15 pedestrians arrive, 10
of them pass the crosswalk directly while the other ﬁve pedestrians wait a while before crossing. For vehicles, the vehicular
delay depends on the remaining crossing time of the pedestrian when the vehicle arrives, which is related to the pedestrian
walking speed, the length of the crosswalk, and the position of the pedestrian when the vehicle arrives. As shown in Fig. 3(b),
for pedestrian 1, the corresponding vehicle does not yield because the vehicle is very close to the crosswalk. The pedestrian
only waits 1.6 s. For pedestrians 2 and 3, they have to wait because the ﬁrst two vehicles they met do not yield. However, the
following vehicle yields, and then they can cross. The subsequent vehicles have to queue and wait until they ﬁnish the cross-
ing. For pedestrian 5, he rejects the gap he meets. However, the vehicle decides to yield him. So, after waiting a short time,
the pedestrian notices the vehicle yield (the vehicle slows down and the gap increases) and then starts to cross. It shows that
the proposed model can describe the interaction between VY and PGA behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks.
4.3. Empirical data validation
The proposed model converged the probabilistic models of VY and PGA into the car-following model, which were devel-
oped separately in previous studies (Malenje et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). However, the accuracy of the separate behavioral
models may not lead to the accuracy of the combined model. Therefore, in this section, the proposed model is validated using
empirical data. The data were collected from two unsignalized midblock crosswalks in Shanghai, China, in a peak hour, as
shown in Fig. 4. Since the previous studies covered the study area, the same parameter values for VY and PGA were used
without recalibration. The arrival time and the time of starting to cross of pedestrians were recorded. Then, the average wait-
ing time of pedestrians can be measured as the indicator to evaluate the veridicality of the model. The average value of the
ten runs with 7200 s for each run is used as the result of simulation for comparison. The comparison results are shown in
Table 2. One can ﬁnd that the maximum relative error is less than 10%. Moreover, the Two-Related-Samples nonparametric
test (Wilcoxon signed-rank) shows that there is no signiﬁcant difference (p > 0.05) between the estimated and the surveyed
values, as shown in Table 3. It indicates that the ability of the proposed model to estimate the operational performance at the
unsignalized midblock crosswalk is acceptable.

Moreover, the simulation will run 10 times for each scenario tested below.

the two sub-ﬁgures in Fig. 5, one can ﬁnd that the vehicular delay increases more rapidly than the pedestrian waiting time
with the increase of the volume. It is because the arriving vehicles have to queue once the vehicle in front yields and cannot
depart simultaneously after pedestrians complete crossing. Additionally, the queue clearance takes time as vehicles have to
restart and move according to the car-following model. With the increase in volume, more vehicles have to wait in the
queue. Consequently, the vehicular delay increases more rapidly with the increase of the volume between 400 and 600
(see Fig. 5(a)). However, as shown in Fig. 5(b), pedestrians can depart simultaneously once a gap is available. Therefore,
although the increase of the vehicular volume decreases the available gap for crossing, the inﬂuence on the waiting time
of the pedestrians is slight if the driver willing to yield.
For the given parameters (collected from the ﬁeld survey in Shanghai), the vehicles tend to yield. Under this condition, the
difference in delay between vehicles and pedestrians is great, especially when the vehicular volume is high. The signal con-
trol is recommended when the vehicular volume is larger than 480 veh/h by using the vehicular delay of 20 s as a threshold,
which is the criteria of the LOS (level of service) B for vehicles at signalized intersections (TRB, 2010). Please note that in
Fig. 5, the volume is constant for each scenario. However, under the condition of the trafﬁc demand ﬂuctuation, e.g., during
different times of day, it is unsuitable for controlling the crosswalk by ﬁxed signal timing. A signal control with pushbutton is
an alternative scheme to increase ﬂexibility (Kutela & Teng, 2020).
5.2. Effect of the pedestrian volume
The presence and number of pedestrians at an unsignalized crosswalk do affect the response from drivers and inﬂuence
their decision to either yield or not. In this test, the inputs for the volume of the pedestrians measured as the cumulative
number of pedestrians per hour (ped/h) was set from 100 to 800 at 100 intervals. Other parameters are constant, which
is the same as that in Section 4. The result of this test shows that the delay of vehicles increases more rapidly with the
increase in the volume of pedestrians, as shown in Fig. 6(a). Notably, the more the pedestrian volume is, the more time
the crosswalk will be occupied by pedestrians. Then, vehicles have to remain in the queue for a longer time. Moreover, after

However, for pedestrians, the waiting time is inversely proportional to the volume of pedestrians. From pedestrians’ per-
spective, with the increase in the pedestrian volume, more time is available for use by pedestrians, as shown in Fig. 6(b). It is
because if one gap is accepted, the crosswalk will be occupied by the pedestrians for the duration of the crossing move, and
in the process, vehicles have to slow down and yield to pedestrians to use the crosswalk. If the pedestrians arrive continu-
ously, the crosswalk will be occupied for a long time. Therefore, along the same line as the vehicular volume analysis, the
signal control is recommended when the pedestrian volume is larger than 500 ped/h by using the vehicular delay of 20 s
as a threshold. E.g., the crosswalk can be controlled by the signal at the school area because the extreme peak crossing
demand may occur during the morning and afternoon school time (Tang, Xie, Wang, & Zhang, 2019). It can also ensure
the safety of the pedestrian.
5.3. Effect of the number of trafﬁc lanes
The number of lanes affects the distance and duration that pedestrians need to traverse a crosswalk. In this test, the num-
ber of trafﬁc lanes used was set to be 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6, when other parameters are constant, which is the same as that in
Section 4. Fig. 7 shows that, for the pedestrians, the increase in the number of trafﬁc lanes makes the waiting time to increase
rapidly. It is because of the sharp decline in the available gap. Moreover, the possibility that vehicles on all the trafﬁc lanes
yielding also drops substantially with the increase in the number of trafﬁc lanes. Therefore, it is challenging for pedestrians
to ﬁnd a safe gap for crossing under the condition that the number of trafﬁc lanes is high. According to Guo, Wang, Guo, Jiang,
and Bubb (2012), most pedestrians can endure up to a 50 s wait. Therefore, it is not appropriate to have an unsignalized
crosswalk set on a segment with more than 4 lanes. Instead, a central island should be added to reduce the number of trafﬁc
lanes the pedestrians have to cross in one time. Alternatively, the crosswalk can be signalized to clarify the right of way.
For the vehicles, the delay also increases with the increase in the number of trafﬁc lanes. On average, 4 s increases in
vehicular delay for one trafﬁc lane increase. It is because the more the number of trafﬁc lanes is, the longer time the pedes-
trians have to spend on the crossing.

6. Conclusion
This study explored the interaction of vehicles and pedestrians at the unsignalized midblock crosswalks, where the two
parties share sections of a roadway with or without conﬂicts in a trafﬁc stream based on the car-following theory. It involved
developing a microscopic trafﬁc ﬂow model considering the behaviors of both the driver and pedestrians as represented in
established logit models of VY and PGA. The numerical results show that the proposed model can describe the interaction
and qualitatively analyze its effects on trafﬁc ﬂow. The operational performance of the unsignalized midblock crosswalks
under different trafﬁc and geometric conditions were explored based on the sensitivity analyses. The following are the
key ﬁndings of the study.
(1) For pedestrians, the waiting time increases with the increase in the number of lanes and vehicular volume but
decreases with the pedestrian volume. The number of lanes is the critical factor affecting the waiting time of the
pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks.
(2) The vehicular delay increases with the growth of the vehicles’ volume, the pedestrians’ volume, and the number of
lanes. Compared with the pedestrians, the vehicular delay increases more rapidly than the pedestrian waiting time
with the increase of the vehicles’ volume, pedestrians’ volume.
(3) The signal control is recommended when the pedestrian waiting time and vehicular delay is high. It is related to the
behavior of drivers and pedestrians. According to the parameters surveyed in this study, the signal control is recom-
mended when the number of trafﬁc lanes at the midblock crosswalk is more than 4.
This study explored the effect of vehicle-pedestrian interaction at a midblock unsignalized crosswalk where pedestrian
crossing is legal though not controlled. The interaction under the condition the crosswalk is not set at the segment is worth
to do further study in the future because the illegal crossing (cross at the point without the crosswalk) is typical in devel-
oping countries. It is a tradeoff whether a midblock crosswalk should be set. Moreover, the combined consideration of the
midblock crosswalk and the adjacent intersections can be the direction of the future study. The arrival distribution of vehi-
cles is strongly affected when the distance to adjacent intersections is considered.
